as the rental income from the buy-to-let property is currently received jointly, each of you declares £10,500 per annum on your respective self-assessment tax returns. for emma, this income is taxed at the higher rate of 40%, resulting in a tax liability of approximately £4,200 on her share. for daniel, however, the position is considerably less favourable. as an additional rate taxpayer with a marginal rate of 45%, daniel's share of the rental income attracts a tax liability of approximately £4,725, meaning that the same £10,500 of income costs daniel almost £500 more in tax than it costs emma simply by virtue of the difference in their respective marginal rates. taken together, the current joint arrangement generates a combined income tax liability on the rental income of approximately £8,925 per annum.#N##N##N##N#where a property is held in joint names, hmrc's default position is to tax the rental income on a 50/50 basis regardless of the actual financial contribution each party makes. howe

can i gift partial ownership and use multiple annual exempt amounts over several years? yes, in principle. each disposal is a separate event for cgt, so gifting 20% to a child this tax year and another 20% next year uses each year's annual exempt amount. however, hmrc may apply the linked transactions rule under tcga 1992 s19 where the staged gifts are part of an overall arrangement, treating them as a single transaction. the relief works cleanly where there is genuine commercial reason for staging (e.g., the child reaching majority, financial need at specific points). it is more vulnerable to challenge where staged purely for cgt advantage.
